summary: The Docker-based reproduction demonstrates that modifying the path.imagemagick configuration to a malicious value enables arbitrary command execution. The PoC sets the path to /bin/sh;-c;echo EVOHUNT_POC_SUCCESS and verifies the injected command executes successfully.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should inject a malicious command via the ImageMagick path configuration and execute it, producing the proof string EVOHUNT_POC_SUCCESS in /tmp/pwned.txt, demonstrating arbitrary command execution with application privileges.
actual_observed_result: The PoC successfully demonstrated command injection. The malicious path /bin/sh;-c;echo EVOHUNT_POC_SUCCESS was processed by ProcessBuilder, which executed the shell command and created /tmp/pwned.txt containing the proof string EVOHUNT_POC_SUCCESS.
verification_method: The verify.sh script checks for the existence of /tmp/pwned.txt and validates it contains the exact proof string EVOHUNT_POC_SUCCESS. This distinguishes successful command injection from build failures or unrelated errors. The proof string is unique to this PoC and cannot appear through normal application operation.
evidence_produced_by_run: Proof file /tmp/pwned.txt was successfully created by the injected command
The file contains the exact proof string EVOHUNT_POC_SUCCESS
The command executed with the privileges of the Docker container, simulating application-level access
No legitimate ImageMagick conversion was required - the injection occurred before any actual conversion
The exploit demonstrates that any file conversion trigger (image upload, profile picture, PDF, recording) would execute the injected command

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh copies the target repository to a temporary checkout and mounts it read-only at /target-repo in the container.
